critic_raw:
The design justifies a stratified random split by arguing that 'temporal autocorrelation in patient outcomes is not significant enough to necessitate a purely chronological split.' This is offered as an assumption, not a tested property. In the same sentence, the design claims the split 'ensures that models learn patterns that generalize well to unseen data without leaking future information available at discharge.' These two claims are in tension: if temporal autocorrelation is present (even if argued to be small), a random split allows sequences from the same patient's visit history to span train and test boundaries, leaking future visit information that would not be available at the time of the discharge prediction.

The RNN uses 'time-stamped sequences of diagnosis codes, treatment history, and follow-up visits.' The inclusion of 'follow-up visits' as a training feature is a potential post-discharge leakage source: follow-up visits occur after discharge, and if these are included in the feature set without strict temporal truncation at the discharge timestamp, the model is trained with knowledge of what happened after the event it is trying to predict. The design states all features must be available 'at discharge' but does not explicitly confirm that follow-up visit data is excluded.

Recall is listed as the primary metric with the justification of minimizing false negatives among high-risk patients. But recall is a threshold-dependent metric: its value depends on where the classification boundary is set. The design does not specify the threshold selection strategy. If the threshold is chosen post-hoc to maximize recall on the validation set, a model that predicts all patients as high-risk will achieve perfect recall and be selected as the winner — which is operationally meaningless. The design should specify an acceptable precision floor or use a threshold-free metric (PR-AUC) as the primary criterion.

The RNN model has access to visit-level temporal sequences, while the logistic regression baseline uses 'aggregate demographic and clinical variables.' This feature inequality means the comparison tests whether richer granular data (sequences) outperforms coarser aggregated data, not whether the RNN architecture itself is better. A gradient-boosted tree trained on the same aggregate features as logistic regression, or a logistic regression with sequence-derived features, would be needed to isolate the architectural contribution.

The design uses approximately 100,000 patient records over three years. It does not state how many unique patients this represents — if the dataset has high re-admission rates with many visits per patient, the effective number of independent patients may be far smaller (perhaps 30,000-50,000), and the evaluation set may have fewer than 2,000 unique patients, limiting the statistical reliability of metric estimates, especially at subgroup levels.
all_issues_raised: Follow-up visits are included in the RNN feature set without explicit confirmation that they are temporally truncated at discharge; post-discharge visits are direct label leakage if included as training features for a discharge-time prediction task.

Recall as primary metric is threshold-dependent without a specified threshold selection strategy or precision floor; a model predicting all patients as high-risk achieves perfect recall and may be selected as winner during validation, producing a clinically useless outcome.

The RNN-vs-logistic regression comparison confounds data granularity (visit sequences vs. aggregates) with model architecture; any observed advantage cannot be attributed to the RNN's temporal modeling specifically.

The assumption that temporal autocorrelation is 'not significant enough' for a chronological split is asserted without evidence; even modest autocorrelation means a random split places future visit data from the same patient in training, leaking individual patient trajectories.

The effective number of independent patients is unspecified; high re-admission rates common in chronic disease populations could mean 100,000 records represent far fewer unique patients, degrading statistical reliability of metric estimates to the point where observed AUROC differences are insignificant.
all_issues_adjudicated: Follow-up visits are included in the RNN feature set without explicit confirmation that they are temporally truncated at discharge; post-discharge visits are direct label leakage if included as training features for a discharge-time prediction task.

Recall as primary metric is threshold-dependent without a specified threshold selection strategy or precision floor; a model predicting all patients as high-risk achieves perfect recall and may be selected as winner during validation, producing a clinically useless outcome.

The RNN-vs-logistic regression comparison confounds data granularity (visit sequences vs. aggregates) with model architecture; any observed advantage cannot be attributed to the RNN's temporal modeling specifically.

The assumption that temporal autocorrelation is 'not significant enough' for a chronological split is asserted without evidence; even modest autocorrelation means a random split places future visit data from the same patient in training, leaking individual patient trajectories.

The effective number of independent patients is unspecified; high re-admission rates common in chronic disease populations could mean 100,000 records represent far fewer unique patients, degrading statistical reliability of metric estimates to the point where observed AUROC differences are insignificant.
